sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 65-year-old woman with a history of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, presented with shortness of breath, which she describes as a new symptom that started over the weekend.
She reports being unable to walk her dog as far as she used to without feeling short of breath.
She denies coughing, phlegm, fever, body aches, sore throat, and sinus pain.
However, she reports a painful sensation in her chest when swallowing, which is not associated with food getting stuck.
She has been able to drink liquids, including Ensure and milkshakes, but has been taking them less frequently due to the pain.
She is on a combination of cisplatin and paclitaxel chemotherapy, which she started a few days ago, and has been experiencing nausea and fatigue, which have been managed with zofran.
Past medical history:
The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, for which she is undergoing chemotherapy and radiation.
Review of systems:
The patient reports a dry cough, shortness of breath, and painful swallowing, but denies other symptoms.
Current medications:
Cisplatin, paclitaxel, zofran, prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days, lidocaine spray for swallowing pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, and temperature are within normal limits.
Physical exam:

The patient's physical exam is notable for crackles in the lungs bilaterally, which may indicate fluid in the lungs, and erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which may be due to radiation.
The patient's skin exam is also notable for no lower extremity edema.
Test results:
The patient had a chest x-ray that showed mild radiation pneumonitis, which is inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; and painful swallowing due to radiation-induced inflammation of the esophagus.
Plan:
I will continue the patient on her current chemotherapy regimen and radiation doses.
I will prescribe prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in her lungs and help her breathe better.
I will also prescribe lidocaine spray for swallowing pain, which she can use 4 times a day.
The patient will be seen again in two weeks.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in two weeks to assess the effectiveness of the current treatment plan and to make any necessary adjustments.
